audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man inquiring in a tourist center about activities suitable for families.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi. Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: I'd like to find out if you have any excursions suitable for families.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: How about taking your family for a cruise?
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: We have a steamship that takes passengers out several times a day.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: It's over 100 years old.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: The cruise takes place on a steamship.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, steamship has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi. Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: I'd like to find out if you have any excursions suitable for families.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: How about taking your family for a cruise?
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: We have a steamship that takes passengers out several times a day.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: It's over 100 years old.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: That sounds interesting.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: How long is the trip?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: About an hour and a half.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: And don't forget to take pictures of the mountains.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: They're all around you when you're on the boat, and they look fantastic.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK, and I assume there's a cafe or something on board?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: How old are your children?
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Uh. My daughter's fifteen, and my son's 7.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Well, there are various things you can do once you've crossed the lake to make a day of it.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: One thing that's very popular is a visit to the country farm.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: You're met off the boat by the farmer, and he'll take you to the holding pens where the sheep are kept.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Children love feeling them.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: My son would love that.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: He really likes animals.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Well, there's also a 40-minute trek round the farm on a horse, if he wants.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Do you think he'd manage it?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: He hasn't done that before.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Sure, it's suitable for complete beginners.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Ah, good.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: And again visitors are welcome to explore the farm on their own, as long as they take care to close gates and so on.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: There are some very beautiful gardens along the side of the lake, which also belong to the farm.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: They'll be just at their best now.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: You could easily spend an hour or two there.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK, well, that all sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: And can we get lunch there?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: You can, and it's very good, though it's not included in the basic cost.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: You pay when you get there.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, is there anything else to do over on that side of the lake?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Well, what you can do is take a bike over on the ship, and then go on a cycling trip.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: There's a trail there called the Back Road.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: You could easily spend three or four hours exploring it, and the scenery's wonderful.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: They'll give you a map when you get your ticket for the cruise, there's no extra charge.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: What's the trail like in terms of difficulty?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Quite challenging in places, it wouldn't be suitable for your 7 year old, it needs someone who's got a bit more experience.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Hmm, well my daughter loves cycling, and so do I.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: So maybe the two of us could go, and my wife and son could stay on the farm.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: That might work out quite well, uh, but we don't have bikes here.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Is there somewhere we could rent them?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Yes, there's a place here in the city.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: It's called Ratchesons.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: I'll just make a note of that.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Uh. How do you spell it?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: RATCHESONS.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: It's just by the cruise ship terminal.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: You'd also need to pick up a repair kit for the bike from there to take along with you, and you'd need to take along a snack and some water.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: It would be best to get those in the city.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Fine, that shouldn't be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: And I assume I can rent a helmet from the bike place.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Sure, you should definitely get that.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: It's a great ride, but you want to be well prepared, because it's very remote.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: You won't see any shops round there or anywhere to stay, so you need to get back in time for the last boat.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Uh. So what sort of prices are we looking at here?
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Let's see, that would be one adult and one child for the cruise with Farm tour.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: That's 117 dollars, and an adult and a child for the cruise only, so that's $214 altogether.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Oh, wait a minute.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: How old did you say your daughter was?
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Then I'm afraid it's $267, because she has to pay the adult fare, which is $75, instead of the child fare, which is $22 sorry about that.
audioscript.speaker: TC EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: That's OK.
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: Er, so how do...
audioscript.speaker: VISITOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a restaurant manager talking to a small group of new staff who are starting work as kitchen assistants in a restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: My name's Joy Parkins, and I'm the restaurant manager.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: And I understand that none of you've had any previous experience as kitchen assistants.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Well, you might be feeling a bit nervous now, but most of our kitchen assistants say they enjoy the work.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: OK, they might get shouted at sometimes, but it's nothing personal.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: And they're pleased that they have so many different things to do, which means they never get bored.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: And I'll tell you straight away that if you do well, we might think about moving you up and giving you some more responsibility.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Right, well, you've all shown up on time, which is an excellent start.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Now I'm glad to see none of you have unsuitable footwear, so that's good.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: You need to be careful as the floors can get very wet and slippery.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Those of you with long hair have got it well out of the way, but some of you'll need to remove your rings and bracelets, just put them somewhere safe for today.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: And remember to leave them at home tomorrow, as they can be a safety hazard.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Now it's going to be a busy day for you all today.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: We don't have any tables free for this evening.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: And only a few for lunch.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Fortunately, we've got our head chef back.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: He was away on holiday all last week, which meant the other chefs had extra work.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Now I'll tell you a bit more about the job in a minute, but first some general regulations for all of you, whatever your age.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: There's some equipment you mustn't use until you've been properly trained, like the waste disposal system for example, for health and safety reasons.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Then I think there are two of you here who are under 18.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: That's Emma and Jake, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Right, so for you two the meat slicer is out of bounds, and of course none of you are allowed to use the electric mixer until you've been shown how it works.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Now, you may have heard that this can be a stressful job, and I have to say that can be true.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: You'll be working an 8-hour day for the first week.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Though you'll have the chance to do overtime after that as well, if you want to.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: But however long the hours are, you'll get a break in the middle.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: What you will find is that you're on your feet all day long, lifting and carrying.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: So, if you're not fit now, you soon will be.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: You'll find you don't have much chance to take it easy, when someone tells you to do something, you need to do it straight away.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: But at least we do have a very efficient air conditioning system, compared with some kitchens.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, let me tell you about some of the people you need to know.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: So, as I said, I'm Joy Parkins, and I decide who does what during the day and how long they work for.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: I'll be trying to get you to work with as many different people in the kitchen as possible, so that you learn while you're on the job.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: One person whose name you must remember is David Field.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: If you injure yourself at all, even if it's really minor, you must report to him, and he'll make sure the incident is recorded.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: And you get the appropriate treatment.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: He's trained to give basic treatment to staff himself, or he'll send you off somewhere else if necessary.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Then there's Dexter Wills.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: He's the person you need to see, if you smash a plate or something like that.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: Don't just leave it and hope no one will notice.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: It's really important to get things noted and replaced, or there could be problems later.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: And finally, there's Mike Smith.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: He's the member of staff who takes care of all the stores of perishables.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: So, if you notice we're getting low in flour or sugar or something, make sure you let him know, so he can put in an order.
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: OK, now the next thing...
audioscript.speaker: JOY PARKINS

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a restaurant manager talking to a small group of new staff who are starting work as kitchen assistants in a restaurant.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Good morning everyone.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: My name's Joy Parkins, and I'm the restaurant manager.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: And I understand that none of you've had any previous experience as kitchen assistants.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Well, you might be feeling a bit nervous now, but most of our kitchen assistants say they enjoy the work.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: OK, they might get shouted at sometimes, but it's nothing personal.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: And they're pleased that they have so many different things to do, which means they never get bored.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: And I'll tell you straight away that if you do well, we might think about moving you up and giving you some more responsibility.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Right, well, you've all shown up on time, which is an excellent start.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Now I'm glad to see none of you have unsuitable footwear, so that's good.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: You need to be careful as the floors can get very wet and slippery.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Those of you with long hair have got it well out of the way, but some of you'll need to remove your rings and bracelets, just put them somewhere safe for today.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: And remember to leave them at home tomorrow, as they can be a safety hazard.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Now it's going to be a busy day for you all today.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: We don't have any tables free for this evening.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: And only a few for lunch.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Fortunately, we've got our head chef back.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: He was away on holiday all last week, which meant the other chefs had extra work.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Now I'll tell you a bit more about the job in a minute, but first some general regulations for all of you, whatever your age.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: There's some equipment you mustn't use until you've been properly trained, like the waste disposal system for example, for health and safety reasons.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Then I think there are two of you here who are under 18.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: That's Emma and Jake, isn't it?
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Right, so for you two the meat slicer is out of bounds, and of course none of you are allowed to use the electric mixer until you've been shown how it works.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Now, you may have heard that this can be a stressful job, and I have to say that can be true.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: You'll be working an 8-hour day for the first week.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Though you'll have the chance to do overtime after that as well, if you want to.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: But however long the hours are, you'll get a break in the middle.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: What you will find is that you're on your feet all day long, lifting and carrying.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: So, if you're not fit now, you soon will be.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: You'll find you don't have much chance to take it easy, when someone tells you to do something, you need to do it straight away.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: But at least we do have a very efficient air conditioning system, compared with some kitchens.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, let me tell you about some of the people you need to know.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: So, as I said, I'm Joy Parkins, and I decide who does what during the day and how long they work for.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: I'll be trying to get you to work with as many different people in the kitchen as possible, so that you learn while you're on the job.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: One person whose name you must remember is David Field.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: If you injure yourself at all, even if it's really minor, you must report to him, and he'll make sure the incident is recorded.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: And you get the appropriate treatment.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: He's trained to give basic treatment to staff himself, or he'll send you off somewhere else if necessary.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Then there's Dexter Wills.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: He's the person you need to see, if you smash a plate or something like that.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: Don't just leave it and hope no one will notice.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: It's really important to get things noted and replaced, or there could be problems later.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: And finally, there's Mike Smith.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: He's the member of staff who takes care of all the stores of perishables.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: So, if you notice we're getting low in flour or sugar or something, make sure you let him know, so he can put in an order.
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: OK, now the next thing...
utterances.speaker: JOY PARKINS

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: According to the manager, what do most people like about the job of kitchen assistant?
The manager is concerned about some of the new staff's
The manager says that the day is likely to be busy for kitchen staff because
options: the restaurant is almost fully booked.

items.title: Only kitchen staff who are 18 or older are allowed to use

body.title: According to the manager, which TWO things can make the job of kitchen assistant stressful?
options: They have to follow orders immediately.
They may not be able to take a break.

items: Joy Parkins  <input>
David Field  <input>
Dexter Wills <input>
Mike Smith   <input>
body.title: Restaurant staff

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students called Trudie and Stewart, who are both studying to be librarians, discussing a paper that they are going to write together.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 23.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 23.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, Stewart, we need to start planning our paper on public libraries.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Have you thought of an angle yet?
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Well, there's so much we could look into, how libraries have changed over the centuries, for instance, or how different countries organize them, what do you think, Trudie?
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Maybe we should concentrate on this country and try to relate the changes in libraries to external developments, like the fact that far more people can read than a century ago, and that the local population may speak lots of different languages.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: We could include something about changes in the source of funding too.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Yes, but remember we're only supposed to write a short paper, so it's probably best if we don't go into funding in any detail.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Right, well, shall we just brainstorm a few ideas to get started?
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: OK, we obviously need to look at the impact of new technology, particularly the internet.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Now that lots of books have been digitalized, people can access them from their own computers at home.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: And if everyone did that, libraries would be obsolete.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: But the digitalized books that are available online for free are mostly out of copyright, aren't they?
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: And copyright in this country lasts for 70 years after the author dies.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: So you won't find the latest best-seller or up-to-date information.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: That's an important point.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Anyway, I find it hard to concentrate when I'm reading a long text on a screen.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: I'd much rather read a physical book, and it takes longer to read on a screen.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Oh, I prefer it.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: I suppose it's just a personal preference.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Hmm, I expect libraries will go on evolving in the next few years.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Some have already become centers where community activities take place.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Like local clubs meeting there, I think that'll become even more common.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: I'd like to think so, and that they'll still be serving their traditional function, but I'm not so sure.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: There are financial implications after all.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: What I'm afraid will happen is that books and magazines will all disappear, and there'll just be rows and rows of computers.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: They won't look anything like the libraries we're used to.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Well, we'll see.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 24 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 24 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've just had an idea.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Why don't we make an in-depth study of our local public library as background to our paper?
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Yes, that'd be interesting, and raise all sorts of issues.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Let's make a list of possible things we could ask about, then work out some sort of structure.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: For instance, um, we could interview some of the staff, and find out whether the library has its own budget, or if that's controlled by the local council.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: And what their policies are.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: I know they don't allow food, but I'd love to find out what types of noise they ban.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: There always seems to be a lot of talking, but never music.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Hmm, I don't know if that's a policy or it just happens.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Ah. I've often wondered.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Then there are things like how the library is affected by employment laws.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: I suppose there are rules about working hours, facilities for staff and so on.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Then there are other issues relating to the design of the building and how customers use it, like what measures does the library take to ensure their safety?
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: They'd need floor coverings that aren't slippery,
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: and emergency exits for instance.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Oh, and another thing, there's the question of the kind of insurance the library needs to have, in case anyone gets injured.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Yes, that's something else to find out.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: You know something I've often wondered.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: What's that?
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Well, you know they've got an archive of local newspapers going back years.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Well, next to it, they've got the diary of a well known politician from the late 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: I wonder why it's there.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Do you know what his connection was with this area?
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: No idea.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Let's add it to our list of things to find out.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Oh, I've just thought - you know people might ask in the library about local organizations, like sports clubs?
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Well, I wonder if they keep a database, or whether they just look online.
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: I quite fancy finding out what the differences are between a library that's open to the public and one that's part of a museum, for example.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: They must be very different.
audioscript.speaker: STEWART

audioscript.text: Hmm, then something else I'd like to know is...
audioscript.speaker: TRUDIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What will be the main topic of Trudie and Stewart's paper?
options: how public library services are organised in different countries
how changes in society are reflected in public libraries
how the funding of public libraries has changed

options: they may take a long time to read.
they can be difficult to read.

its policy regarding noise of various kinds
how it's affected by laws regarding all aspects of <input>
how the design needs to take the <input> of customers into account
what <input> is required in case of accidents
why a famous person's <input> is located in the library
whether it has a <input> of local organisations
how it's different from a library in a <input>
body.title: Study of local library: possible questions

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture for business students about business values.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In public discussion of business, we take certain values for granted.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, I'm going to talk about four of them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Collaboration, hard work, creativity, and excellence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. Most people would say they're all good things.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm going to suggest that's an over-simple view.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The trouble with these values is that they're theoretical concepts removed from the reality of day-to-day business, uh, pursue values by all means.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But be prepared for what may happen as a result.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can actually cause damage, which is not at all the intention.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Business leaders generally try to do the right thing, but all too often the right thing backfires.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If those leaders adopt values without understanding and managing the side effects that arise.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The values can easily get in the way of what's actually intended.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, so the first value I'm going to discuss is collaboration.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. Let me give you an example, on a management training course I once attended.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We were put into groups, and had to construct a bridge across a stream, using building blocks that we were given.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The rule was that everyone in the team had to move at least one building block during the construction.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was intended to encourage teamwork, but it was really a job best done by one person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The other teams tried to collaborate on building the structure, and descended into confusion with everyone getting in each other's way.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our team leader solved the challenge brilliantly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: She simply asked everyone in the team to move a piece a few centimeters to comply with the rule, and then let the person in the team with an aptitude for puzzles like this build it alone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We finished before any other team.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: My point is that the task wasn't really suited to team working, so why make it one?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Teamwork can also lead to inconsistency, a common cause of poor sales.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the case of a smartphone that a certain company launched.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One director wanted to target the business market, and another demanded it was aimed at consumers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The company wanted both directors to be involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So gave the product a consumer-friendly name, but marketed it to companies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The result was that it met the needs of neither group.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It would have been better to let one director or the other have his way, not both.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now industriousness or hard work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's easy to mock people who say they work hard.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. After all, a hamster running around in a wheel is working hard, and getting nowhere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of course, hard work is valuable, but only when properly targeted, otherwise it wastes the resources that companies value most, time and energy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that's bad for the organization.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's a management model that groups people according to four criteria, clever, hard-working, stupid, and lazy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here, lazy means having a rational determination not to carry out unnecessary tasks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It doesn't mean trying to avoid work altogether.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most people display two of these characteristics, and the most valuable people are those who are both clever and lazy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They possess intellectual clarity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they don't rush into making decisions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They come up with solutions to save the time and energy spent by the stupid and hard-working group.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead of throwing more man hours at a problem, the clever and lazy group looks for a more effective solution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Next, we come to creativity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This often works well, creating an attention grabbing TV commercial for example, might lead to increased sales.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it isn't always a good thing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some advertising campaigns are remembered for their creativity without having any effect on sales.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This happened a few years ago with the launch of a chocolate bar.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Subsequent research showed that plenty of consumers remembered the adverts, but had no idea what was being advertised.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The trouble is that the creator derives pleasure from coming up with the idea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And wrongly assumes the audience for the campaign will share that feeling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A company that brings out thousands of new products may seem more creative than a company that only has a few, but it may be too creative.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And make smaller profits.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Creativity needs to be targeted to solve a problem that the company has identified.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Just coming up with more and more novel products isn't necessarily a good thing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally, excellence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We all know companies that claim they strive for excellence, but it takes a long time to achieve excellence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In business, being first with a product is more profitable than having the best product.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A major study of company performance compared pioneers - that is, companies bringing out the first version of a particular product - with followers, the companies that copied and improved on that product.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The study found that the pioneers commanded an average market share of 29 percent, while the followers achieved less than half that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Only 13 percent, even though their product might have been better.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Insisting on excellence in everything we do is time consuming, wastes energy, and leads to losing out on opportunities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sometimes, second-rate work is more worthwhile than excellence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: 'Make sure it's excellent' sounds like a good approach to business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the just get started approach is likely to be more successful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture for business students about business values.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In public discussion of business, we take certain values for granted.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today, I'm going to talk about four of them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Collaboration, hard work, creativity, and excellence.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. Most people would say they're all good things.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'm going to suggest that's an over-simple view.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The trouble with these values is that they're theoretical concepts removed from the reality of day-to-day business, uh, pursue values by all means.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But be prepared for what may happen as a result.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They can actually cause damage, which is not at all the intention.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Business leaders generally try to do the right thing, but all too often the right thing backfires.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If those leaders adopt values without understanding and managing the side effects that arise.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The values can easily get in the way of what's actually intended.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, so the first value I'm going to discuss is collaboration.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. Let me give you an example, on a management training course I once attended.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We were put into groups, and had to construct a bridge across a stream, using building blocks that we were given.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The rule was that everyone in the team had to move at least one building block during the construction.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was intended to encourage teamwork, but it was really a job best done by one person.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The other teams tried to collaborate on building the structure, and descended into confusion with everyone getting in each other's way.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Our team leader solved the challenge brilliantly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: She simply asked everyone in the team to move a piece a few centimeters to comply with the rule, and then let the person in the team with an aptitude for puzzles like this build it alone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We finished before any other team.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: My point is that the task wasn't really suited to team working, so why make it one?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Teamwork can also lead to inconsistency, a common cause of poor sales.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the case of a smartphone that a certain company launched.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One director wanted to target the business market, and another demanded it was aimed at consumers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The company wanted both directors to be involved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So gave the product a consumer-friendly name, but marketed it to companies.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The result was that it met the needs of neither group.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It would have been better to let one director or the other have his way, not both.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now industriousness or hard work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's easy to mock people who say they work hard.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. After all, a hamster running around in a wheel is working hard, and getting nowhere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of course, hard work is valuable, but only when properly targeted, otherwise it wastes the resources that companies value most, time and energy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And that's bad for the organization.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's a management model that groups people according to four criteria, clever, hard-working, stupid, and lazy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Here, lazy means having a rational determination not to carry out unnecessary tasks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It doesn't mean trying to avoid work altogether.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most people display two of these characteristics, and the most valuable people are those who are both clever and lazy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They possess intellectual clarity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they don't rush into making decisions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They come up with solutions to save the time and energy spent by the stupid and hard-working group.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Instead of throwing more man hours at a problem, the clever and lazy group looks for a more effective solution.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Next, we come to creativity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This often works well, creating an attention grabbing TV commercial for example, might lead to increased sales.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it isn't always a good thing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some advertising campaigns are remembered for their creativity without having any effect on sales.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This happened a few years ago with the launch of a chocolate bar.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Subsequent research showed that plenty of consumers remembered the adverts, but had no idea what was being advertised.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The trouble is that the creator derives pleasure from coming up with the idea.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And wrongly assumes the audience for the campaign will share that feeling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A company that brings out thousands of new products may seem more creative than a company that only has a few, but it may be too creative.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And make smaller profits.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Creativity needs to be targeted to solve a problem that the company has identified.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Just coming up with more and more novel products isn't necessarily a good thing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And finally, excellence.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We all know companies that claim they strive for excellence, but it takes a long time to achieve excellence.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In business, being first with a product is more profitable than having the best product.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A major study of company performance compared pioneers - that is, companies bringing out the first version of a particular product - with followers, the companies that copied and improved on that product.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The study found that the pioneers commanded an average market share of 29 percent, while the followers achieved less than half that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Only 13 percent, even though their product might have been better.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Insisting on excellence in everything we do is time consuming, wastes energy, and leads to losing out on opportunities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Sometimes, second-rate work is more worthwhile than excellence.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: 'Make sure it's excellent' sounds like a good approach to business.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the just get started approach is likely to be more successful.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

The speaker's team won because they reduced collaboration.

items.title: Collaboration

body.title: Four business values
